title: Beginner 20: Reasoning Data Orientation
goal: Build the vocabulary for post-training reasoning data before diving into recipes.

entry.title: Datasheets for datasets
entry.one_line_summary: Introduces dataset datasheets: a structured documentation template for provenance, composition, collection process, recommended uses, and limitations.
entry.why_it_matters: It gives reasoning-data releases a minimum disclosure standard before anyone reuses prompts, traces, labels, rewards, or benchmark items.
entry.audit_focus: check links, lineage, verifier, split, and contamination

entries.title: Data statements for natural language processing

entry.title: Data statements for natural language processing
entry.one_line_summary: Proposes data statements for NLP datasets, foregrounding language, speaker/community provenance, annotation context, and intended deployment boundaries.
entry.why_it_matters: Reasoning-data users need this lens when a corpus mixes web text, synthetic questions, human annotations, or domain-specific tasks whose population assumptions affect generalization.
entry.audit_focus: check links, lineage, verifier, split, and contamination

entries.title: Training language models to follow instructions with human feedback

entry.title: Training language models to follow instructions with human feedback
entry.one_line_summary: InstructGPT establishes the demonstration, preference-comparison, reward-model, and PPO pipeline that many later post-training recipes inherit.
entry.why_it_matters: It is the alignment-data baseline for separating supervised demonstrations, pairwise preferences, learned rewards, and policy optimization in later reasoning models.
entry.audit_focus: check links, lineage, verifier, split, and contamination

entries.title: Chain-of-thought prompting elicits reasoning in large language models

entry.title: Chain-of-thought prompting elicits reasoning in large language models
entry.one_line_summary: Shows that few-shot natural-language rationales can elicit multi-step reasoning behavior from sufficiently large language models.
entry.why_it_matters: It is the conceptual bridge from answer-only prompts to trace-shaped reasoning examples, which later become SFT, distillation, filtering, and verifier targets.
entry.audit_focus: check links, lineage, verifier, split, and contamination

entries.title: Training verifiers to solve math word problems

entry.title: Training verifiers to solve math word problems
entry.one_line_summary: Introduces GSM8K and trains verifier models to rank model-generated math solutions by likely correctness.
entry.why_it_matters: It anchors answer-level math reasoning data as a pair of problem, solution, and verifier-selection signal, anticipating RLVR and reward-model workflows.
entry.audit_focus: check links, lineage, verifier, split, and contamination

entry.title: STaR: Bootstrapping reasoning with reasoning
entry.one_line_summary: STaR iteratively generates rationales, keeps examples whose final answers are correct, and fine-tunes on the accepted reasoning traces.
entry.why_it_matters: It is a compact recipe for self-improving reasoning data: model traces become training data only after answer-based filtering.
entry.audit_focus: check links, lineage, verifier, split, and contamination

entry.title: Self-Instruct: Aligning language models with self-generated instructions
entry.one_line_summary: Self-Instruct bootstraps instruction-following data by having a model generate instructions, inputs, and outputs, then filtering low-quality or duplicate examples.
entry.why_it_matters: It is the canonical self-generated instruction-data recipe that later reasoning datasets adapt for prompt sourcing and synthetic expansion.
entry.audit_focus: check links, lineage, verifier, split, and contamination

entry.title: Direct preference optimization: Your language model is secretly a reward model
entry.one_line_summary: DPO trains a policy directly from preference pairs by turning the reward-model objective into a supervised contrastive optimization problem.
entry.why_it_matters: It shows that pairwise preference data can shape post-training behavior without deploying a separate learned reward model during optimization.
entry.audit_focus: check links, lineage, verifier, split, and contamination

entry.title: Let's Verify Step by Step
entry.one_line_summary: Provides step-level human labels for mathematical reasoning traces and trains process reward models to identify correct intermediate reasoning.
entry.why_it_matters: It is the process-supervision anchor for moving from answer-level math rewards to step-level feedback in reasoning-model training and evaluation.
entry.data_object: step-level labels and final answers; process: step, label, solution trace; offline math reasoning traces
entry.feedback_verifier: process reward model trained from step labels
entry.audit_focus: check links, lineage, verifier, split, and contamination

entry.title: GSM8K: Grade School Math 8K
entry.one_line_summary: Canonical grade-school math benchmark with natural-language word problems, worked solutions, and final numeric answers.
entry.why_it_matters: It remains a compact sanity check for answer-verifiable reasoning data, verifier reranking, SFT, and RLVR-style math training.
entry.data_object: natural-language solution with final numeric answer; process: question, solution, final answer; offline math benchmark
entry.feedback_verifier: answer extraction and arithmetic correctness checks
entry.audit_focus: answer extraction errors, contamination through benchmark reuse

entry.title: Measuring mathematical problem solving with the MATH dataset
entry.one_line_summary: Introduces MATH, a competition-style math benchmark with challenging problems, subject categories, and step-by-step solutions.
entry.why_it_matters: MATH became a central answer-verifiable surface for evaluating and training advanced mathematical reasoning beyond grade-school word problems.
entry.audit_focus: check links, lineage, verifier, split, and contamination

entry.title: HumanEval: Hand-Written Evaluation Set
entry.one_line_summary: HumanEval provides hand-written Python programming problems with unit tests for executable code-generation evaluation.
entry.why_it_matters: It made unit-test execution a standard verifier for code reasoning, pass@k reporting, and later code-data filtering recipes.
entry.data_object: Python function completion; process: prompt, canonical solution, unit tests; Python execution harness
entry.audit_focus: public benchmark contamination, unit-test coverage gaps

entry.title: SWE-bench: Can language models resolve real-world GitHub issues?
entry.one_line_summary: SWE-bench turns real GitHub issues into repository-level repair tasks evaluated by applying patches and running tests.
entry.why_it_matters: It is the agent/environment anchor where the reasoning-data object includes repository state, issue text, actions, patches, and test-backed outcomes.
entry.audit_focus: check links, lineage, verifier, split, and contamination

entry.title: RewardBench: Evaluating Reward Models for Language Modeling
entry.one_line_summary: RewardBench evaluates reward models on prompt/chosen/rejected trios spanning chat, reasoning, and safety, including structured preference failures.
entry.why_it_matters: It helps readers test whether a reward signal generalizes beyond helpfulness style into subtle factual, reasoning, refusal, and safety preferences.
entry.data_object: pairwise or scalar reward decisions; process: prompt, chosen/rejected response, reward model score; offline preference benchmark
entry.audit_focus: check links, lineage, verifier, split, and contamination

entry.title: HealthBench
entry.one_line_summary: HealthBench evaluates healthcare conversations with physician-written, conversation-specific rubrics across safety, accuracy, communication, and domain contexts.
entry.why_it_matters: It is a high-stakes example of judgment-required reasoning data where rubric design matters more than exact-match scoring.
entry.data_object: response with rubric/judge evaluation; process: prompt, response, rubric dimension; offline health evaluation benchmark
entry.audit_focus: check links, lineage, verifier, split, and contamination

entry.title: LiveBench: A challenging, contamination-free benchmark for large language models
entry.one_line_summary: LiveBench is a frequently updated, contamination-limited benchmark that uses recent sources and objective scoring across math, code, reasoning, language, instruction following, and data analysis.
entry.why_it_matters: It gives reasoning-data readers a benchmark-refresh pattern for separating real progress from memorized or stale evaluation items.
entry.audit_focus: check links, lineage, verifier, split, and contamination

entry.title: OpenThoughts: Data recipes for reasoning models
entry.one_line_summary: OpenThoughts studies open data recipes for reasoning models through large public reasoning datasets and many controlled pipeline experiments.
entry.why_it_matters: It is one of the most useful open references for how question sourcing, filtering, teacher traces, and answer generation choices change downstream reasoning performance.
entry.data_object: reasoning traces and final answers; process: question, reasoning trace, answer; offline reasoning corpus
entry.feedback_verifier: filters, benchmark feedback, and recipe ablations
entry.audit_focus: check links, lineage, verifier, split, and contamination

entry.title: DeepSeek-R1
entry.one_line_summary: DeepSeek-R1 reports a reasoning-model post-training recipe centered on reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards, cold-start data, and distillation.
entry.why_it_matters: It is a frontier reference for public RLVR discussion, showing how verifiable tasks, reward design, and distillation shape reasoning behavior.
entry.audit_focus: check links, lineage, verifier, split, and contamination

entry.title: s1: Simple Test-Time Scaling
entry.one_line_summary: s1 curates a small s1K reasoning dataset and studies budget forcing as a simple way to scale test-time reasoning compute.
entry.why_it_matters: It is a useful counterpoint to massive-data recipes: careful small-set curation plus inference-budget control can materially change reasoning performance.
entry.audit_focus: check links, lineage, verifier, split, and contamination

entries.title: CodeT: Code Generation with Generated Tests

entry.title: CodeT: Code Generation with Generated Tests
entry.one_line_summary: CodeT uses model-generated tests and execution agreement to rerank code-generation samples.
entry.why_it_matters: It makes test generation itself part of the reasoning-data object, not just an external evaluation script.
entry.data_object: programming prompt, candidate program set, generated test cases, execution matrix, and selected solution.; process: prompt, candidate solution, generated test; code execution harness across programming benchmarks.
entry.feedback_verifier: generated tests plus dual execution agreement among code samples.
entry.audit_focus: Generated tests can share model biases with generated solutions., Agreement among wrong programs can select a wrong answer., Extra generated-test budget can dominate pass@1 comparisons.

entry.title: Competition-Level Code Generation with AlphaCode
entry.one_line_summary: AlphaCode shows how competitive-programming data, hidden tests, massive sampling, and filtering become a programmatic reasoning stack.
entry.why_it_matters: It is a top-team, top-venue anchor for treating code solutions as verifier-checked samples rather than plain text completions.
entry.data_object: problem statement, generated program, sampled candidate set, and submission verdict.; process: problem statement, generated code sample, cluster or filter score; Codeforces-like competitive programming judge with hidden tests.
entry.feedback_verifier: program behavior under test cases and contest verdicts.
entry.audit_focus: Large sampling budgets can dominate model quality., Public contest solutions can contaminate training or evaluation if dates are not controlled., Passing hidden tests does not expose failed attempts or reasoning traces.

entries.title: BigCodeBench: Benchmarking Code Generation with Diverse Function Calls and Complex Instructions

entry.title: BigCodeBench: Benchmarking Code Generation with Diverse Function Calls and Complex Instructions
entry.one_line_summary: BigCodeBench evaluates code generation on API-heavy tasks with executable tests and branch-coverage-aware checks.
entry.why_it_matters: It expands the code-verification pool from short standalone functions to realistic library and function-call compositions.
entry.data_object: instruction or docstring, required function calls, generated code, and test results.; process: instruction, library/API requirements, generated code; Python execution harness covering diverse libraries and function calls.
entry.feedback_verifier: unit tests with high branch coverage for each task.
entry.audit_focus: Library version drift can change accepted behavior., High branch coverage still may miss semantic edge cases., API-heavy tasks can conflate retrieval/API knowledge with reasoning.

entry.title: LiveCodeBench: Holistic and contamination-free evaluation of large language models for code
entry.one_line_summary: LiveCodeBench continuously collects recent programming problems to evaluate code generation, execution, repair, and test-output prediction under lower contamination risk.
entry.why_it_matters: It gives code-reasoning evaluation a moving-time-window design, making it harder to confuse memorized public problems with genuine coding capability.
entry.data_object: program submission or code-related output evaluated by tests or task-specific checks.; process: problem release date, platform, prompt, generated code, tests, pass/fail result, evaluation window.; code execution and benchmark leaderboard infrastructure.
entry.feedback_verifier: programmatic tests and task-specific correctness checks.
entry.audit_focus: Live benchmarks still become stale after release., Execution settings can affect pass/fail outcomes., Public leaderboard feedback can shape future training.

entry.title: SWE-bench: Can language models resolve real-world GitHub issues?
entry.one_line_summary: SWE-bench turns real GitHub issues into repository-level repair tasks evaluated by applying patches and running tests.
entry.why_it_matters: It is the agent/environment anchor where the reasoning-data object includes repository state, issue text, actions, patches, and test-backed outcomes.
entry.audit_focus: check links, lineage, verifier, split, and contamination

entry.title: Introducing SWE-bench Verified
entry.one_line_summary: SWE-bench Verified is a human-filtered 500-instance subset of SWE-bench designed to reduce ambiguous, unsolvable, or incorrectly tested software-engineering tasks.
entry.why_it_matters: It is now a central coding-agent evaluation surface because the data object contains issue text, repository state, proposed patch, and test-backed success criteria.
entry.data_object: patch diff applied to a repository plus test execution results.; process: repository, issue, base commit, patch, FAIL TO PASS tests, PASS TO PASS tests, human validation notes.; Dockerized repository checkout and unit-test harness.
entry.feedback_verifier: post-patch unit tests plus human filtering of task validity.
entry.audit_focus: Leaderboard scores can depend strongly on scaffold design., Tests may not cover all acceptable patches., Public benchmark tasks can become training targets over time.

entries.title: SWE-Lancer: Can Frontier LLMs Earn $1 Million from Real-World Freelance Software Engineering?

entry.title: SWE-Lancer: Can Frontier LLMs Earn $1 Million from Real-World Freelance Software Engineering?
entry.one_line_summary: SWE-Lancer evaluates real freelance software-engineering tasks with verified tests, managerial labels, and payout-weighted scores.
entry.why_it_matters: It extends Track 03 from repository issues to economically grounded software tasks with both tests and human decision labels.
entry.data_object: task statement, code context, generated patch or decision, test result, and dollar-value mapping.; process: freelance task, repository/context, agent patch; Dockerized software-engineering benchmark with public Diamond split.
entry.feedback_verifier: triple-verified end-to-end tests for IC tasks and human-manager labels for management tasks.
entry.audit_focus: Tests can underspecify intended behavior., Dollar-value mapping may not equal benchmark difficulty., Management tasks require judgment labels rather than pure programmatic checks.

entries.title: MLE-bench: Evaluating Machine Learning Agents on Machine Learning Engineering

entry.title: MLE-bench: Evaluating Machine Learning Agents on Machine Learning Engineering
entry.one_line_summary: MLE-bench evaluates agents on Kaggle-style ML engineering competitions with executable workflows and leaderboard metrics.
entry.why_it_matters: It expands Track 03 from code snippets to full ML engineering episodes where score, compute budget, and contamination are explicit.
entry.data_object: competition task, dataset, experiment/code artifacts, submission score, and resource budget.; process: competition id, dataset, agent scaffold; Kaggle-style ML engineering competitions with executable experiments and leaderboard scoring.
entry.feedback_verifier: competition metric compared with Kaggle leaderboard baselines.
entry.audit_focus: Leaderboards can reward overfitting to public competition practices., Resource scaling can dominate model comparisons., Kaggle data licenses vary by competition.

entries.title: PaperBench: Evaluating AI's Ability to Replicate AI Research

entry.title: PaperBench: Evaluating AI's Ability to Replicate AI Research
entry.one_line_summary: PaperBench evaluates research-agent replication attempts with hierarchical rubrics and judge/human baselines.
entry.why_it_matters: It marks the point where Track 03 verification becomes a hybrid of executable artifacts, experiments, rubrics, and judge calibration.
entry.data_object: paper replication task, codebase/experiment artifacts, rubric item, judge score, and human baseline.; process: target paper, replication subtask, generated code; AI research replication environment with hierarchical rubrics.
entry.feedback_verifier: author co-developed rubrics and an LLM-based judge benchmarked against human grading.
entry.audit_focus: LLM judge scores can drift or encode rubric shortcuts., Replicating a paper mixes reading, coding, experimentation, and environment setup., Target papers may have public code that changes task difficulty.

entries.title: DS-1000: A Natural and Reliable Benchmark for Data Science Code Generation

entry.title: DS-1000: A Natural and Reliable Benchmark for Data Science Code Generation
entry.one_line_summary: DS-1000 evaluates data-science code generation with tests and API constraints over realistic Python library tasks.
entry.why_it_matters: It broadens Track 03 beyond toy function synthesis toward dependency-sensitive executable code tasks.
entry.data_object: natural-language data-science task with Python code solution.; process: prompt, required library, generated code; Python data-science libraries such as NumPy and Pandas.
entry.audit_focus: Python library versions can change accepted behavior., API constraints may reward surface compliance rather than robust code., Public benchmark reuse can train models on test cases.

entry.title: ScienceAgentBench: Toward Rigorous Assessment of Language Agents for Data-Driven Scientific Discovery
entry.one_line_summary: ScienceAgentBench turns peer-reviewed data-driven discovery tasks into executable Python-program agent evaluations.
entry.why_it_matters: It adds a high-quality scientific-workflow surface where verification includes code execution, domain metrics, and expert validation.
entry.data_object: scientific task, input data, generated program, execution output, metric score, and cost.; process: publication-derived task, input data, program file; scientific data-analysis code execution environment.
entry.feedback_verifier: execution results, task metrics, expert validation, and cost measurements.
entry.audit_focus: Program execution may pass while scientific interpretation is wrong., Expert validation can be expensive and non-uniform., Self-debug attempts add test-time compute that must be reported.

entry.title: SpreadsheetBench: Towards Challenging Real World Spreadsheet Manipulation
entry.one_line_summary: SpreadsheetBench evaluates real-world spreadsheet manipulation with multiple test files per instruction.
entry.why_it_matters: It broadens programmatic verification to office/data work where hidden test workbooks play the role of unit tests.
entry.data_object: spreadsheet instruction, input workbook, generated manipulation/formula, and test-case score.; process: forum question, spreadsheet file, instruction; spreadsheet manipulation environment with multiple workbook test cases.
entry.feedback_verifier: online-judge-like spreadsheet tests across varied values/files.
entry.audit_focus: Formula solutions can overfit visible examples., Spreadsheet formatting and hidden state can affect scoring., Forum tasks may contain ambiguous user intent.

entry.title: KernelBench: Can LLMs Write Efficient GPU Kernels?
entry.one_line_summary: KernelBench scores generated GPU kernels by both functional correctness and measured speedup.
entry.why_it_matters: It adds a high-value code surface where the verifier is not only pass/fail correctness but also measured performance.
entry.data_object: workload specification, generated kernel, functional test result, speedup, and fast p score.; process: PyTorch workload, generated kernel, compilation result; GPU execution and profiling environment for kernel correctness and speed.
entry.feedback_verifier: functional correctness tests plus runtime speedup threshold.
entry.audit_focus: Correct kernels can still be slower than baseline., Hardware-specific performance can vary widely., Compilation success can mask numerical precision bugs.

entries.title: Mathematical discoveries from program search with large language models

entry.title: Mathematical discoveries from program search with large language models
entry.one_line_summary: FunSearch pairs an LLM with executable evaluators and evolutionary search to discover verifier-scored programs.
entry.why_it_matters: It expands programmatic verification beyond benchmark answering: the reusable record is a candidate program, evaluator score, search budget, and audit trail.
entry.data_object: executable candidate program scored by a domain-specific evaluator.; process: prompt with sampled programs, generated function, execution result; executable program-search environment for math and combinatorial optimization.
entry.feedback_verifier: deterministic evaluator that scores candidate programs.
entry.audit_focus: The evaluator can become the bottleneck or encode a narrow objective., Generated programs that score well may be hard to generalize beyond the tested inputs., Search cost and evaluator parallelism must be reported before comparing systems.

entries.title: AlphaEvolve: A coding agent for scientific and algorithmic discovery

entry.title: AlphaEvolve: A coding agent for scientific and algorithmic discovery
entry.one_line_summary: AlphaEvolve uses LLM-generated code variants, evaluator feedback, and evolutionary search to discover improved algorithms.
entry.why_it_matters: It broadens Track 03/10 from answering benchmark problems to optimizing executable artifacts under explicit evaluator budgets.
entry.data_object: candidate program, evaluator score, mutation lineage, and accepted improvement.; process: initial program, candidate edit, evaluator score; sandboxed code execution and domain-specific evaluator functions.
entry.feedback_verifier: one or more programmatic evaluators scoring correctness and objective value.
entry.audit_focus: Evaluator design can overfit to narrow objective functions., Search budget and parallel evaluator count can dominate results., Accepted programs may be correct for benchmarked cases but brittle outside them.

entries.title: Solving olympiad geometry without human demonstrations

entry.title: Solving olympiad geometry without human demonstrations
entry.one_line_summary: AlphaGeometry trains on synthetic geometry proofs and uses symbolic proof feedback to solve olympiad geometry problems.
entry.why_it_matters: It is one of the clearest top-team examples of programmatically verifiable reasoning data generated without human demonstrations.
entry.data_object: theorem premises, auxiliary construction, symbolic deduction trace, and proof result.; process: theorem premises, conclusion, auxiliary construction; geometry-specific symbolic deduction engine and IMO-AG benchmark.
entry.feedback_verifier: symbolic geometry prover verifies whether the theorem conclusion is reached.
entry.audit_focus: Geometry-specific language may not transfer to Lean or broader mathematics., Symbolic engine coverage can hide unrepresented problem types., Search budget and translation choices affect comparison to human olympiad performance.

entries.title: Gold-medalist Performance in Solving Olympiad Geometry with AlphaGeometry2

entry.title: Gold-medalist Performance in Solving Olympiad Geometry with AlphaGeometry2
entry.one_line_summary: AlphaGeometry2 expands synthetic geometry data and search to gold-medalist-level geometry-solving performance.
entry.why_it_matters: It gives Track 03 and Track 10 a clean example where synthetic proof data, symbolic verification, and search budget are all first-class metadata.
entry.data_object: geometry statement, auxiliary construction, symbolic proof path, and solve result.; process: problem statement, representation-language translation, auxiliary construction; geometry representation language plus symbolic deduction engine.
entry.feedback_verifier: symbolic theorem-proving engine and solved/unsolved benchmark outcome.
entry.audit_focus: Geometry-specific representation limits claims outside plane geometry., Search budget and translation coverage can dominate reported gold-medalist comparisons., Synthetic proof language may bias the model toward verifier-specific constructions.

entry.title: HOList: An Environment for Machine Learning of Higher-Order Theorem Proving
entry.one_line_summary: HOList exposes higher-order theorem proving as a machine-learning environment with formal checker feedback.
entry.why_it_matters: It is an early bridge from static proof benchmarks to proof-state/action trajectories with a hard formal verifier.
entry.data_object: proof goal, tactic/action, proof state transition, and theorem-proving result.; process: goal statement, available premises, tactic action; HOL Light theorem prover exposed as an RL-style environment.
entry.feedback_verifier: HOL Light kernel and theorem-prover state transitions.
entry.audit_focus: HOL Light-specific tactics may not transfer to Lean or Coq., Proof-search budget strongly affects success rates., Library theorem overlap can inflate evaluation if not versioned.

entry.title: miniF2F: A cross-system benchmark for formal olympiad-level mathematics
entry.one_line_summary: miniF2F is a cross-system formal mathematics benchmark for comparing theorem provers across Lean, Metamath, Isabelle, and HOL Light targets.
entry.why_it_matters: It is a compact formal-proof evaluation surface where the verifier is not a text judge but a proof assistant accepting or rejecting a proof.
entry.data_object: formal proof accepted by a target proof assistant.; process: formal system, theorem statement, split, generated proof/tactics, verifier result.; Lean, Metamath, Isabelle, and HOL Light style theorem proving environments.
entry.audit_focus: A theorem can be easier in one formal system than another., Search budget can dominate model differences., Forks can drift from the original benchmark.

entry.title: LeanDojo: Theorem proving with retrieval-augmented language models
entry.one_line_summary: LeanDojo releases an open Lean theorem-proving environment, benchmark, and retrieval-augmented prover pipeline.
entry.why_it_matters: It turns formal proof work into reusable agent data: repository state, accessible premises, proof states, tactics, retrieval context, and verifier feedback are all part of the record.
entry.data_object: Lean tactic sequence or proof script checked by Lean.; process: repository commit, theorem, proof state, premises, retrieved context, tactic, Lean feedback, split.; Lean proof assistant environment and traced math-library repositories.
entry.audit_focus: A prover can rely on retrieval leakage., Lean version drift can break proofs., Premise accessibility rules can change task difficulty.

entry.title: ProofNet: Autoformalizing and Formally Proving Undergraduate-Level Mathematics
entry.one_line_summary: ProofNet pairs informal undergraduate mathematics with Lean formal statements for autoformalization and proving evaluation.
entry.why_it_matters: It gives Track 03 a clean data object for the informal-to-formal bridge before full proof-generation systems are trained.
entry.data_object: natural-language theorem, natural-language proof, and formal Lean theorem statement.; process: informal theorem, informal proof, Lean statement; Lean 3 theorem-proving environment.
entry.feedback_verifier: Lean type checker / proof assistant acceptance for formal statements and proofs.
entry.audit_focus: Statement-only formalization does not guarantee complete proof ability., Lean 3 dependency versions affect acceptance., Textbook source coverage may skew toward standard undergraduate domains.

entry.title: PutnamBench: Evaluating Neural Theorem-Provers on the Putnam Mathematical Competition
entry.one_line_summary: PutnamBench formalizes Putnam competition problems across proof assistants for checker-backed theorem-proving evaluation.
entry.why_it_matters: It gives Track 03 a high-difficulty proof benchmark where correctness is a proof-assistant predicate rather than textual grading.
entry.data_object: informal theorem, formal statement, proof assistant target, and prover output.; process: Putnam problem, formal statement, proof assistant; Lean 4, Isabelle, and Coq/Rocq theorem-proving environments.
entry.audit_focus: Formalization choices can change problem difficulty., Proof assistant versions and libraries can drift., Cross-system comparisons can hide differences in available tactics and libraries.

entry.title: FrontierMath: A Benchmark for Evaluating Advanced Mathematical Reasoning in AI
entry.one_line_summary: FrontierMath provides expert-written advanced mathematics problems with verifier-oriented evaluation and contamination controls.
entry.why_it_matters: It gives the representative pool a high-difficulty math surface where automatic verification and contamination policy are central fields.
entry.data_object: advanced math problem with final answer or verifier-checkable target.; process: problem statement, mathematical domain, difficulty tier; held-out advanced mathematics benchmark with automated answer verification where possible.
entry.feedback_verifier: automated answer checks and expert-vetted benchmark criteria.
entry.audit_focus: Automated verification may favor final-answer tasks over proof quality., Held-out access policy limits reproducibility., Models can overfit once examples or solution patterns circulate.

entry.title: Omni-MATH: A Universal Olympiad Level Mathematic Benchmark For Large Language Models
entry.one_line_summary: Omni-MATH adds a large olympiad-level math benchmark with human annotation and answer-verification challenges.
entry.why_it_matters: It gives Track 03/10 a harder math evaluation surface for reasoning models after easier math benchmarks saturate.
entry.data_object: competition math problem, annotated solution/answer, difficulty and sub-domain metadata.; process: problem, sub-domain, difficulty; olympiad-level math benchmark and answer-grading pipeline.
entry.feedback_verifier: reference answers, answer normalization, and human annotation.
entry.audit_focus: Answer normalization may fail for proof-style or expression-equivalent answers., Public competition problems can be contaminated., Difficulty labels may not correspond to verifier reliability.

entry.title: OlympiadBench: A Challenging Benchmark for Promoting AGI with Olympiad-Level Bilingual Multimodal Scientific Problems
entry.one_line_summary: OlympiadBench packages bilingual multimodal olympiad math and physics problems with expert annotations and scoring.
entry.why_it_matters: It captures cases where programmatic answer checking, expert rubric scoring, and multimodal reasoning must be audited together.
entry.data_object: problem statement, optional image, step-level annotation, reference answer, and score.; process: problem, modality, subject; bilingual multimodal math/physics benchmark.
entry.feedback_verifier: answer key, expert annotations, and scoring methodology.
entry.audit_focus: Some items need rubric judgment rather than exact answer checking., Multimodal diagrams introduce OCR/vision failure separate from reasoning., Public olympiad problems and solutions can leak into training data.

entries.title: Is Your Code Generated by Large Language Models Really Correct

entry.title: Is Your Code Generated by Large Language Models Really Correct? Rigorous Evaluation of Large Language Models for Code Generation
entry.one_line_summary: EvalPlus strengthens HumanEval/MBPP-style code evaluation with generated and mutation-based tests.
entry.why_it_matters: It directly audits a central Track 03 assumption: passing public unit tests is not the same as functional correctness.
entry.data_object: code-generation task, candidate solution, original tests, and augmented tests.; process: prompt, candidate program, generated tests; Python execution harness for HumanEval+ and MBPP+.
entry.feedback_verifier: expanded unit-test suites generated by LLM and mutation strategies.
entry.audit_focus: Generated tests can still miss semantic edge cases., Public augmented tests may cause future contamination., Stricter tests can alter leaderboard rankings without changing task coverage.

title: Representative 30: RLVR and Test-Time Compute
goal: Compare verifier rewards, repeated sampling, tree search, process rewards, RL scaling, and failure audits.

entries.title: Training verifiers to solve math word problems

entry.title: Training verifiers to solve math word problems
entry.one_line_summary: Introduces GSM8K and trains verifier models to rank model-generated math solutions by likely correctness.
entry.why_it_matters: It anchors answer-level math reasoning data as a pair of problem, solution, and verifier-selection signal, anticipating RLVR and reward-model workflows.
entry.audit_focus: check links, lineage, verifier, split, and contamination

entries.title: Self-consistency improves chain of thought reasoning in language models

entry.title: Self-consistency improves chain of thought reasoning in language models
entry.one_line_summary: Self-consistency samples multiple chain-of-thought reasoning paths and chooses the answer that is most consistent across samples.
entry.why_it_matters: It is the classic test-time compute baseline for reasoning: performance can improve by spending more samples and marginalizing over traces without changing training data.
entry.data_object: multiple rationales and final answers for the same prompt.; process: sampling temperature, number of paths, answer extraction, aggregation rule.; benchmark prompting setup.
entry.feedback_verifier: majority or marginalization over sampled answers.
entry.audit_focus: More samples can amplify benchmark-specific shortcuts., Aggregation does not guarantee step faithfulness., Unmatched inference budgets can make methods look better than they are.

entry.title: Tree of Thoughts: Deliberate Problem Solving with Large Language Models
entry.one_line_summary: Tree of Thoughts turns reasoning into a search tree of intermediate thoughts with self-evaluation and backtracking.
entry.why_it_matters: It anchors Track 10's search-budget vocabulary before RLVR-era reasoning models popularized test-time compute scaling.
entry.data_object: search tree of intermediate thoughts, state evaluations, and final answer.; process: thought state, branch score, search step; prompt-level search scaffold over LLM-generated thoughts.
entry.feedback_verifier: self-evaluation, task success checks, or environment-specific scoring.
entry.audit_focus: Search budget can dominate comparisons with chain-of-thought baselines., Self-evaluation can reward plausible but wrong branches., Task-specific scaffolds may not transfer to programmatic RLVR settings.

entry.title: Let's Verify Step by Step
entry.one_line_summary: Provides step-level human labels for mathematical reasoning traces and trains process reward models to identify correct intermediate reasoning.
entry.why_it_matters: It is the process-supervision anchor for moving from answer-level math rewards to step-level feedback in reasoning-model training and evaluation.
entry.data_object: step-level labels and final answers; process: step, label, solution trace; offline math reasoning traces
entry.feedback_verifier: process reward model trained from step labels
entry.audit_focus: check links, lineage, verifier, split, and contamination

entry.title: Math-Shepherd
entry.one_line_summary: Rollout-value supervision method that assigns process rewards to intermediate math reasoning steps.
entry.why_it_matters: It is the clearest bridge between final-answer verifiers and step-level PRM data: the label is not a human judgment but a rollout-derived estimate of whether a partial step can still reach the right answer.
entry.data_object: step-level rollout-value labels; process: reasoning step, rollout result, process reward score; offline math reasoning traces
entry.audit_focus: rollout policy strength can leak into labels, step rewards may favor locally plausible continuations, generated solutions can inherit base-model shortcuts

entry.title: OmegaPRM: Improve Mathematical Reasoning in Language Models by Automated Process Supervision
entry.one_line_summary: Automated process-supervision recipe that uses search to locate first errors and generate PRM training signals without human labels.
entry.why_it_matters: Automated process-supervision recipe that uses search to locate first errors and generate PRM training signals without human labels.
entry.data_object: process supervision annotations; process: partial reasoning prefix, first-error signal, positive/negative step examples; offline math search tree
entry.audit_focus: check links, lineage, verifier, split, and contamination

entries.title: Let's reward step by step: Step-Level reward model as the Navigators for Reasoning

entry.title: Let's reward step by step: Step-Level reward model as the Navigators for Reasoning
entry.one_line_summary: Uses step-level reward models as navigators for math and code reasoning during inference.
entry.why_it_matters: It makes the verifier-scaling question concrete: the reward model is not only trained, it actively steers the search path.
entry.data_object: partial reasoning path, step score, search decision, and final answer.; process: prompt, reasoning step, PRM score; PRM-guided inference over math and code tasks.
entry.audit_focus: Greedy PRM navigation can prefer locally plausible but globally wrong steps., Automatically generated code-step rewards can inherit executor artifacts., PRM gains should be separated from extra inference budget.

entries.title: V-STaR: Training Verifiers for Self-Taught Reasoners

entry.title: V-STaR: Training Verifiers for Self-Taught Reasoners
entry.one_line_summary: V-STaR trains a verifier from both correct and incorrect self-generated solutions, then uses it for candidate selection.
entry.why_it_matters: It makes failed rollouts a reusable data asset for verifier learning rather than discarded self-training waste.
entry.data_object: problem, candidate solution, correctness label, verifier preference, and selected answer.; process: problem, generated solution, correctness label; self-training loop over math and code reasoning benchmarks.
entry.feedback_verifier: DPO-trained verifier using both correct and incorrect self-generated solutions.
entry.audit_focus: Verifier may learn style cues instead of correctness., Incorrect samples are useful but can bias preference data., Best-of-N gains depend on candidate diversity and sample budget.

entries.title: Generative Verifiers: Reward Modeling as Next-Token Prediction

entry.title: Generative Verifiers: Reward Modeling as Next-Token Prediction
entry.one_line_summary: Generative Verifiers trains reward models with next-token prediction and scales verification with verifier-side sampling.
entry.why_it_matters: It adds the verifier itself to the Track 10 compute-scaling object instead of treating it as a fixed scorer.
entry.data_object: problem, candidate solution, verification rationale, verifier judgment, and best-of-N selection.; process: problem, candidate solution, verification rationale; best-of-N reasoning evaluation with verifier-based selection.
entry.feedback_verifier: generative reward model trained with next-token prediction over verification data.
entry.audit_focus: Verifier rationales can be plausible but wrong., Majority voting over verifier samples can hide calibration failures., Best-of-N improvements depend on both policy and verifier budgets.

entry.title: ReST-MCTS*: LLM Self-Training via Process Reward Guided Tree Search
entry.one_line_summary: ReST-MCTS* uses process-reward-guided tree search to collect reasoning traces and value targets for self-training.
entry.why_it_matters: It is a clean Track 10 example where search traces, process rewards, and training data are the same reusable artifact.
entry.data_object: problem, search tree, intermediate step, process value, selected trace, and final answer.; process: problem, tree node, reasoning step; process-reward-guided Monte Carlo tree search over reasoning steps.
entry.feedback_verifier: oracle final-answer checks used to infer process rewards through tree search.
entry.audit_focus: Tree-search budget can dominate comparison to best-of-N., Incorrect intermediate steps may be selected if final-answer credit is noisy., Process value estimates inherit final-answer oracle limitations.

entry.title: Scaling LLM Test-Time Compute Optimally can be More Effective than Scaling Model Parameters
entry.one_line_summary: Studies when adaptive test-time compute and verifier-guided search can beat simply scaling model parameters.
entry.why_it_matters: It gives Track 10 a direct framework for auditing inference-budget claims instead of treating every improvement as better reasoning data.
entry.data_object: prompt, candidate response or reasoning path, verifier score, and compute allocation.; process: prompt difficulty, candidate response, process reward score; verifier-guided inference-time search over reasoning tasks.
entry.feedback_verifier: dense process-based verifier rewards and outcome success checks.
entry.audit_focus: Compute-optimal gains can disappear if prompt difficulty is misestimated., Dense verifier rewards may encode task-specific shortcuts., FLOPs-matched comparisons require transparent accounting of verifier and search cost.

entries.title: Inference Scaling Laws: An Empirical Analysis of Compute-Optimal Inference for Problem-Solving with Language Models

entry.title: Inference Scaling Laws: An Empirical Analysis of Compute-Optimal Inference for Problem-Solving with Language Models
entry.one_line_summary: Inference Scaling Laws compares model size and inference algorithms under explicit compute budgets.
entry.why_it_matters: It gives Track 10 a second compute-optimal anchor focused on algorithms and FLOPs accounting, not only verifier rewards.
entry.data_object: problem, model size, inference strategy, compute budget, candidate answers, and correctness.; process: problem, model size, inference algorithm; compute-budgeted inference strategies for problem solving.
entry.audit_focus: FLOPs accounting can omit verifier/search overhead., Small-model advantage depends on task distribution and algorithm implementation., Tree-search gains may not transfer without reliable intermediate scoring.

entry.title: How Do Large Language Monkeys Get Their Power (Laws)?
entry.one_line_summary: Explains repeated-sampling scaling by separating per-problem success curves from aggregate heavy-tailed difficulty.
entry.why_it_matters: It helps readers audit pass@k and coverage claims by asking whether gains come from broad improvement or a few easy-to-sample tasks.
entry.data_object: problem, per-attempt success probability, aggregate coverage curve, and scaling-law fit.; process: problem id, single-attempt success probability, number of attempts; tasks with verifiable success under repeated sampling.
entry.feedback_verifier: task-specific correctness checks or benchmark success predicates.
entry.audit_focus: Aggregate power laws can hide per-problem exponential behavior., Heavy-tailed difficulty can make extrapolations brittle., Verifier availability determines whether extra samples can be converted into solved tasks.

entries.title: How Do Large Language Monkeys Get Their Power

entry.title: How Do Large Language Monkeys Get Their Power (Laws)?
entry.one_line_summary: Explains repeated-sampling scaling by separating per-problem success curves from aggregate heavy-tailed difficulty.
entry.why_it_matters: It helps readers audit pass@k and coverage claims by asking whether gains come from broad improvement or a few easy-to-sample tasks.
entry.data_object: problem, per-attempt success probability, aggregate coverage curve, and scaling-law fit.; process: problem id, single-attempt success probability, number of attempts; tasks with verifiable success under repeated sampling.
entry.feedback_verifier: task-specific correctness checks or benchmark success predicates.
entry.audit_focus: Aggregate power laws can hide per-problem exponential behavior., Heavy-tailed difficulty can make extrapolations brittle., Verifier availability determines whether extra samples can be converted into solved tasks.

entry.title: s1: Simple Test-Time Scaling
entry.one_line_summary: s1 curates a small s1K reasoning dataset and studies budget forcing as a simple way to scale test-time reasoning compute.
entry.why_it_matters: It is a useful counterpoint to massive-data recipes: careful small-set curation plus inference-budget control can materially change reasoning performance.

entry.title: ToolSandbox: A Stateful, Conversational, Interactive Evaluation Benchmark for LLM Tool Use Capabilities
entry.one_line_summary: ToolSandbox evaluates tool agents through stateful conversations, user simulation, and milestone-based checks.
entry.why_it_matters: It strengthens the agent benchmark track by making implicit tool state and intermediate milestones part of the feedback contract.
entry.data_object: multi-turn conversation with tool calls, tool state, and milestone checks; process: user turn, agent turn, tool call; stateful tool execution sandbox with user simulator
entry.feedback_verifier: dynamic evaluation of intermediate and final milestones
entry.audit_focus: stateful dependencies can make failures hard to attribute, user simulator behavior can become an implicit reward channel, final success can hide intermediate milestone failures if reporting is incomplete

entry.title: Subliminal Learning
entry.one_line_summary: Subliminal Learning shows that teacher models can transmit behavioral traits through semantically unrelated generated data, even after visible trait references are filtered.
entry.why_it_matters: It is a data-lineage warning for reasoning distillation: synthetic traces may carry hidden model traits that are invisible to content filters.
entry.data_object: generated data plus downstream behavioral evaluation of the student.; process: teacher identity, student base model, visible filtering policy, hidden trait evaluation.; distillation and synthetic-data training pipeline.
entry.audit_focus: Data may look safe while carrying hidden traits., Lineage effects can be invisible from sample inspection., Distillation chains can propagate behavior across model generations.
